title: Spider-Noir Trailer Unleashes Nicolas Cage Against Classic Villains—and a Deep-Cut Foe
summary:
If you were worried that Spider-Noir might be too grounded, a new trailer just proved otherwise. Released at CCXPMX26, the trailer for the upcoming MGM Plus series—starring Nicolas Cage as Ben Reilly (aka The Spider)—is packed with comic-book craziness. We see fights with Sandman, the mob boss Silvermane (played by Brendan Gleeson), and even a deep-cut villain called Megawatt, who has electricity-based powers. The trailer also emphasizes the connection between The Spider and Black Cat, a classic cat burglar.

Spider-Noir is set in the 1930s and follows Ben Reilly, a private investigator with spider-like abilities who had retired but is forced back into action. The show is an adaptation of the Spider-Man Noir comics, which are an alternate-universe take on the Spider-Man mythos. After the first trailer, some fans feared the series would downplay the superpowers and weirdness. But this new footage shows obscure villains and plenty of superpowered action, suggesting the show will embrace its comic-book roots.

One unique feature: the entire series will be released in both black-and-white and color versions, just like the trailers. That means you can watch it twice if it's good! The first episode airs on MGM Plus on May 25, and the full season drops on Prime Video on May 27. Whether you're a die-hard Spider-Man fan or just love Nicolas Cage's over-the-top performances, this show looks like a wild ride.
why_it_matters: Spider-Noir shows how streaming services are investing in niche, artistic versions of popular franchises, giving viewers more genre and visual choices. It also highlights how fan feedback can shape the final product, as creators responded to early concerns about toning down the superhero elements.

questions.correct_answer: The show would downplay superpowers and weirdness

background_read: Spider-Noir is based on the comic series 'Spider-Man Noir,' launched in 2008, which reimagines Spider-Man in a gritty 1930s setting. Nicolas Cage (an Oscar-winning actor known for intense roles in films like 'Leaving Las Vegas' and 'National Treasure') voices the lead character, Ben Reilly.

The show is an alternate-universe adaptation, a common strategy in comics to explore different takes on familiar heroes. It features classic Spider-Man villains like Sandman and Silvermane, as well as the lesser-known Megawatt from a minor comic storyline.

Black Cat (Felicia Hardy) appears as a cat burglar, and Brendan Gleeson (the Irish actor famous for playing Mad-Eye Moody in the 'Harry Potter' series) portrays Silvermane. The series will be released in both black-and-white and color versions, a nod to film noir aesthetics.

The trailer debuted at CCXPMX26, a fan convention in Mexico City. MGM Plus (a streaming service now owned by Amazon) will air the first episode on May 25, with the full season dropping on Prime Video two days later.
Article_Structure: └─ SHOWS: fights with Sandman, Silvermane, Megawatt; connection to Black Cat
BACKGROUND: Set in 1930s; Ben Reilly is a retired PI forced back into action
├─ ADAPTATION: Based on Spider-Man Noir alternate-universe comics
└─ FAN CONCERN: first trailer made some fear lack of superpowers
NEW FOOTAGE: proves show embraces comic-book roots with obscure villains and powers
UNIQUE FEATURE: entire series available in black-and-white or color versions
└─ RELEASE: episode 1 May 25 on MGM Plus; full season May 27 on Prime Video

perspectives.description: Fans of alternate takes and Nicolas Cage will enjoy a creatively free series that embraces weirdness. The black-and-white option adds an artistic layer that could attract cinephiles.
Some may find the style too far from classic Spider-Man, risking alienation of traditional fans. The abundance of obscure villains might confuse casual viewers.

The series fits a trend of noir-themed superhero stories (like 'Gotham' or 'Batman: The Animated Series') and cross-platform releases. Whether it succeeds depends on balancing pulp tone with superhero action.
If successful, Spider-Noir could prove that streaming platforms can profit from gritty, single-season superhero shows. It may influence how other franchises handle alternate-universe spinoffs.
